query: Lipoid Arabinomannan Assay for Tuberculosis Diagnosis

retrieved_docs.title: CNS Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Molecular Diagnostic Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: CSF assays for the MTB cell wall glycolipid lipoarabinomannan show sensitivity comparable to Xpert MTB/RIF, potentially aiding TBM diagnosis in patients with HIV, particularly in resource-limited settings. [35] Lipoarabinomannan detection in CSF demonstrates high TBM specificity. [36] CSF interferon-γ release assays have superior diagnostic accuracy over blood interferon-γ release assays in evaluating TBM. [37] [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Genitourinary Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Identifying Mycobacterium tuberculosis
retrieved_docs.content: Lipoarabinomannan assay: Lipoarabinomannan (LAM) is a glycolipid part of the cell wall of Mtb and can be detected in the urine of patients with active tuberculosis. [89] The lateral flow urine LAM assay is an immunochromatographic assay comprising nano-gold-labeled antibodies attached to LAM, which are captured by immobilized LAM antibodies further along the test strip, forming a visual band. [7] Patients with significant immunosuppression are at the highest risk of disseminated Mtb infection and resultant renal involvement, thereby releasing Mtb LAM glycolipid into the urine. [90] The utility of this assay for diagnosing urogenital tuberculosis has not yet been fully evaluated. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation -- Initial diagnostic testing of TB without drug-resistance testing
retrieved_docs.content: The Determine™ TB LAM lateral flow test (Alere Determine™  TB LAM Ag Alere, MA, USA) detects lipoarabinomannan (LAM), a cell wall component of tuberculosis  which is excreted in the urine of people with active TB, using a simple urine lateral flow assay. [50] In practice, LAM assay sensitivity is relatively low in the general population but improves in PLHIV as immunosuppression increases. The WHO endorses Determine TB LAM use for adults and children with HIV with CD4+ T lymphocyte counts of less than 200 cells/μL and with signs and symptoms of pulmonary or extrapulmonary TB (see Image. Classes of Technologies and Associated Products to Diagnose Tuberculosis). In addition, the assays can be used for PLHIV with CD4+ counts of less than 100 cells/μL irrespective of signs and symptoms of TB. A detailed tabulation of pooled sensitivity and specificity data based on CD4+ count is available. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Miliary Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Immunology-Based Methods
retrieved_docs.content: Serological tests are not advocated for TB detection because of their low sensitivity and specificity. However, adenosine deaminase (ADA) and interferon-γ (IFN-γ) may be used as adjuncts for detecting the presence of MTB in pleural, pericardial, and ascitic fluids. [38] [39] [40] [41] ADA may also be used in diagnosing TBM. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculous Meningitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Various new modalities for testing for antigens and antibodies specific to TBM exist using polymerase chain reaction, but they have not won wide acceptance or utilization due to a lack of access to the testing and high variability in the specificity of the test results. The choice of diagnosis in most cases will depend on the resources available. Despite advances in developing improved and accurate diagnostic modalities, MTB confirmation by culture in the CSF remains the gold standard. [2] [3] [9] Culture allows for the assessment of drug sensitivity results. Drug-resistant MTB carries up to twice the mortality. [9] Other tests that can be utilized are antigen testing in the urine and adenosine deaminase. [10] Recent study results have estimated the sensitivity of several diagnostic tests as follows: Ziehl-Neelsen smear (large volume CSF that is centrifuged to concentrate MTB): 34% GeneXpert: 25% MTB culture: 32% [11] CSF lipoarabinomannan: 22% [12] GeneXpert Ultra: 44% [12]

retrieved_docs.content: The value of IGRA in peritoneal fluid has not been established. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Mycobacterium-Specific Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated adenosine deaminase (ADA) levels in peritoneal fluid are a good diagnostic marker and support a diagnosis of peritoneal TB. [32] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Mycobacterium-Specific Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberculin skin tests and interferon-gamma release assays (IGRA) may be false-negative and are not useful in establishing a diagnosis of active TB. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Genitourinary Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Selecting Specific Diagnostic Testing for Genitourinary Tuberculosis
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberculin skin testing and IGRA are simple and readily available but can only identify latent tuberculosis and are best used for initial screening. [21] [145]

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominal Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Mycobacterium-Specific Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal TB is a paucibacillary disease. [29] AFB stains and cultures have low sensitivity and high specificity; PCR may provide greater sensitivity. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation -- Active Tuberculosis Disease
retrieved_docs.content: A gold standard for diagnosis of active TB is confirmation by culture. However, confirmation is often elusive due to practical difficulties in obtaining adequate sputum, fluid, and tissue specimens, the frequent paucity of organisms, and the organism's slow growth. The diagnosis is often presumptive based on pretest parameters and supporting evidence from imaging, molecular assays, and biomarkers.

retrieved_docs.content: Vasography can be used to visualize obstructions in the vas deferens. [148] [149]

retrieved_docs.title: Genitourinary Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Selecting Specific Diagnostic Testing for Genitourinary Tuberculosis
retrieved_docs.content: Renal nuclear scans are useful in determining kidney function and monitoring the effects of therapy but are very nonspecific.

retrieved_docs.title: Genitourinary Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Selecting Specific Diagnostic Testing for Genitourinary Tuberculosis
retrieved_docs.content: Polymerase chain reaction testing is fast and sensitive for genitourinary tuberculosis but may have false positives; this is best used together with a confirmatory culture or similar test. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Tuberculosis presents a diagnostic challenge to clinicians. The approach to diagnosis depends on whether the patient is being evaluated for latent, active pulmonary, or extrapulmonary TB disease. Signs and symptoms, if present, are nonspecific. The approach to diagnosis depends on prevalence within a population and the resources available within a specific geographic region. [32] [5] Ideally, a combination of radiology, microbiology, molecular methods, biomarkers, and the immunologic response to provocative testing establish a diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1952
retrieved_docs.content: Acute bronchitis is classified under the broader category الْتِهابِ شعبي. Clinically, acute bronchitis is associated with الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ. The condition الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ includes a subcategory called التهاب رئوي وليدي. Both الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ and the measurement حج القحف are linked to a positive فحص التوبركولين, indicating that pulmonary inflammation and head circumference may be related to tuberculin reactivity. This network of relationships highlights the hierarchical classification of respiratory inflammatory disorders and their potential association with immunologic testing results.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1448
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Infectious Disease Terminology incorporates فحص التوبركولين (the tuberculin test) as a specific concept within its scope. فحص التوبركولين is classified as a type of immunizations and screening for infectious disease, highlighting its function in the detection of latent tuberculosis infection. Immunizations and screening for infectious disease serves as a broader category that also encompasses Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings, which are organized under this same parent heading. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how the tuberculin test and various nonspecific laboratory abnormalities are situated within the overarching framework of infectious disease screening and immunization practices, with the pediatric terminology providing a focused context for the tuberculin test.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2518
retrieved_docs.content: Immunizations and screening for infectious disease are linked to فحص التوبركولين إيجابي, indicating that tuberculin testing is a component of infectious‑disease preventive care. In addition, حج القحف is clinically associated with فحص التوبركولين إيجابي, suggesting that measurements of head circumference may be relevant when evaluating a positive tuberculin test, particularly in contexts such as pediatric tuberculosis where cranial growth patterns can reflect disease involvement. Together, these relationships highlight the role of tuberculin screening within broader immunization programs and the potential clinical significance of head‑circumference assessment in patients with a positive tuberculin result.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1010
retrieved_docs.content: The positive tuberculin test (فحص التوبركولين إيجابي) is clinically associated with both appendectomy (استئصال الزائدة) and head circumference (حج القحف), indicating that these clinical parameters may be linked to tuberculin reactivity. In the broader context of patient evaluation, the tuberculin test is mapped to the category of immunizations and screening for infectious disease (immunizations and screening for infectious disease), reflecting its role as a standard screening tool for latent Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection. Within this screening framework, there exists a higher‑level group labeled “Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings,” which is considered a parent category of the infectious‑disease screening activities. A specific child of this group is abnormal chromosome analysis (تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي), representing a particular type of nonspecific abnormal laboratory finding. Together, these relationships illustrate how a positive tuberculin test connects to surgical history, growth measurements, and broader infectious‑disease screening processes, while also being situated within a hierarchy of miscellaneous abnormal laboratory results that include chromosomal analyses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1993
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ). In patients with alcoholic cirrhosis, chest radiographs frequently show atypical patterns that may reflect complications such as pulmonary edema, infections, or pleural effusions, underscoring the importance of evaluating chest imaging when managing this liver disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2573
retrieved_docs.content: Immunizations and screening for infectious disease is a broad clinical category that encompasses specific procedures such as فحص التوبركولين (the tuberculin test). Within this overarching category, there is a subcategory titled Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings, which groups together various atypical laboratory results that do not fit into more specific diagnostic groups. This subcategory further includes the specific assay تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي (abnormal chromosome analysis), illustrating a hierarchical relationship in which general infectious‑disease screening contains a broader set of nonspecific abnormal test findings, and that set contains particular genetic‑chromosome analyses.

The presence of an abnormal chest X‑ray may therefore suggest underlying alcohol‑related pulmonary pathology, such as alcoholic emphysema, and should prompt clinicians to evaluate the patient’s history of alcohol consumption. Recognizing these associations aids in accurate diagnosis and targeted management of patients with suspected alcohol‑related lung disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2868
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcohol consumption (تعاطي الكحول). In patients who consume alcohol, clinicians should consider that pulmonary imaging may reveal atypical or pathological changes, which can stem from alcohol‑related lung injury, aspiration events, infections, or other respiratory complications. Recognizing this association prompts healthcare providers to inquire about alcohol use when interpreting abnormal chest radiographs, to assess for potential alcohol‑related pulmonary disease, and to incorporate appropriate counseling or treatment for alcohol misuse into the overall patient care plan.
